title: Testing
summary: Write unit tests, integration tests, and mocks using Vitest or Jest

sections.title: Testing Is Like a Fire Drill
sections.content: Running tests is like conducting fire drills: you practice scenarios in a controlled environment so you know everything works when it matters. Unit tests verify individual functions in isolation — like testing a single smoke detector. Integration tests verify that components work together — like checking the whole alarm system. End-to-end tests simulate real user scenarios — like a full evacuation drill. Most professional projects aim for a test pyramid: many unit tests, fewer integration tests, and a handful of E2E tests.

sections.title: Unit testing a pricing function
sections.content: Tests a calculateTotal function with normal cases, edge cases, and error cases

sections.title: Mocks and Stubs: Isolating the Code Under Test
sections.content: When testing a function that calls an API or database, you do not want your tests making real network calls. Mocks replace real dependencies with controllable substitutes. A stub returns fixed values. A spy records how a function was called (how many times, with what arguments). In Jest/Vitest, vi.mock() replaces an entire module, and vi.fn() creates a mock function. This lets you test error handling by making the mock throw, or test loading states by making the mock resolve slowly.

sections.title: Testing implementation details instead of behavior
sections.content: The #1 mistake developers make is testing internal implementation rather than public behavior. Wrong: testing that a private method was called or that internal state matches certain values — these tests break on refactoring. Correct: test the public API — given input X, expect output Y. If the internals change but the output is the same, the test should still pass. Refactoring should not require rewriting tests.

sections.title: Your turn
sections.prompt: Write tests for a UserService that has methods: getUser(id) — fetches from an API, and sendWelcomeEmail(user) — calls an email service. Mock the API and email modules so no real calls are made. Test: successful user fetch, fetch returning null (user not found), API throwing an error (network failure), and sendWelcomeEmail being called with the correct user data after fetch.
hints: How do you mock the fetch function (or API module) to return different data per test?
What assertion checks that sendWelcomeEmail was called with the correct argument?
How do you test the error path — what should getUser return or throw when the API fails?
